content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 22, 1998

Fred Galante
Rutan & Tucker, LLP
Post Office Box 1950
Costa Mesa, California 92628-1950

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-98-228

Dear Mr. Galante:

This letter responds to your request on behalf of Councilmember Deyna Hodges for advice about the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

I. QUESTIONS

(A) May Ms. Hodges take part in governmental decisions about selecting the general contractor for the Mid-Valley Parkway Project, where Granite Construction, a significant customer of Ms. Hodges' employer, is bidding for the contract?

(B) Where Granite Construction is a contractor of a developer of a private shopping center project, may Ms. Hodges take part in governmental decisions about the developer's application for a subdivision map and conditional use permit for the project (the "subdivision map decision")?

(C) Given that Wessman Development is an occasional customer of Ms. Hodges' employer, may she take part in governmental decisions about a proposed disposition and development agreement between Wessman Development and the City of Palm Springs Redevelopment Agency (the "Wessman DDA decision")?

___________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-98-228
Page 2

(D) Where Wessman Development owns land which it wishes to develop into a shopping
center, and has applied for a conditional use permit which would allow the development, may
Ms. Hodges take part in governmental decisions about the conditional use permit (the "shopping
center CUP decision")?

II. CONCLUSIONS

Based upon the facts you have presented, Ms. Hodges does not at this time have a conflict
of interest with regard to any of these decisions about which you inquire.

III. FACTS ²

Your office represents the City of Palm Springs ("City") as its City Attorney, and are
authorized by Ms. Hodges to seek advice on her behalf..

Ms. Hodges is an employee of Familian Tile ("Familian"), a plumbing supply business.
She receives a set salary and commission income.

Granite Construction Company ("Granite") is the largest contractor in the Coachella
Valley. Granite annually buys a significant amount of plumbing products from Familian (in the
range of $4,000-$100,000 annually). Granite is not Ms. Hodges' client and Ms. Hodges receives
no commission income from Granite.

Wessman Development ("Wessman"), a Palm Springs developer, has purchased $374
worth of plumbing supplies from Familian in the last 12 months. Ms. Hodges has received a
nominal commission ($5.81) from Wessman for these sales. Although Wessman is an occasional
customer of Familian, you tell us that Wessman tends to use other vendors of plumbing supplies.

A. Facts specific to question (A).

Granite is bidding to be the general contractor on the Mid-Valley Parkway Project, which
is an approximately $15 million dollar regional street project administered by the City as lead
agency. The City must decide whether to award a bid to Granite as the general contractor for the
City. No plumbing supplies will be utilized in the project. The project will be competitively bid,
pursuant to state law.

² The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. This advice is applicable and
confers immunity (see Section 83114) only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct and that all of the
material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, 77.)

File No. A-98-228
Page 3

B. Facts specific to question (B).

Granite is the contractor building parking lot improvements on a private shopping center
project in the City. The developer who has hired Granite is seeking approval from the City of the
subdivision map for the shopping center project, and a conditional use permit ("CUP") for the
development of the shopping center. The developer will purchase plumbing supplies for the
project; it is unknown where the developer will purchase the supplies. Wherever the plumbing
supplies are bought, they will not be bought or utilized by Granite.

C. Facts specific to question (C).

Wessman wishes to enter a disposition and development agreement ("DDA") with the
Palm Springs Redevelopment Agency ("Agency") for development of a shopping center, Plaza
Mercado, and a public parking lot thereon. The Agency plans to provide a subsidy to Wessman
in an amount of $500,000 toward the construction of Plaza Mercado. The City Council will
consider the approval of the DDA between the Agency and Wessman for the project. Plumbing
supplies will be needed for the project, but it is unknown from whom Wessman will purchase the
supplies.

D. Facts specific to question (D).

Wessman seeks to develop a second shopping center on property it owns. The property is
entirely privately owned, and the City will not provide any assistance for the construction of the
shopping center. The City Council must decide whether to issue the CUP necessary for
development of the shopping center. Again, it is unknown where Wessman will purchase the
plumbing supplies for the project.

IV. ANALYSIS

A. Introduction.

The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from economic
interests. The "economic interests" from which conflicts of interest may arise are defined in
Regulations 18703-18703.5. There are five kinds of such economic interests:

File No. A-98-228
Page 4

•     A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $1,000 or more, or, in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Regulation 18703.1);

•     A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $1,000 or more (Regulation 18703.2);

•     A public official has an economic interest in any source of income which aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Regulation 18703.3);

•     A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $290 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Regulation 18703.4);

•     A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family—this is known as the "personal financial effects" rule (Regulation 18703.5).

     As a public official,⁴ Ms. Hodges will have a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to a governmental decision about which you inquire if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on her economic interest(s) which is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.

     The following analysis reflects Commission's recently adopted amendments to the conflict-of-interest regulations. The amendments introduced a standardized, eight-step process for analyzing conflict-of-interest issues; the eight steps are outlined in Regulation 18700.

B. Question (A): the Mid-Valley Parkway project.

     Ms. Hodges has an economic interest in her employer, Familian Title. (Regulation 18703.1(b).) Familian is indirectly involved in the decision about the Mid-Valley Parkway

³ An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10 percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.)

⁴ The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." "Public official," for purposes of the Act, is defined to include every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local agency (with certain exceptions not relevant here). (Section 82048; Regulation 87100.) As a Councilmember, Ms. Hodges is a public official for purposes of the Act.

File No. A-98-228
Page 5

Project.5 The rules for assessing whether a financial effect on an indirectly involved business
entity is material are found in Regulation 18705.1(b). That regulation prescribes alternative
rules, which one applies depends upon the size of the business. We assume that subsection (b)(7)
applies to the plumbing supply business. (You should study Regulation 18705.1(b), and confirm
this assumption based upon your superior access to facts about the business.) Subsection (b)(7)
provides:

"The effect of a decision is material as to a business entity in which an
official has an economic interest if any of the following applies: ...
"(7) For any business entity not covered by subdivisions (b)(1)–(b)(6),
inclusive:
"(A) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the gross
revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or
"(B) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year
in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
"(C) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of
assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more."

The critical question is whether it is substantially likely that the Mid-Valley Parkway
Project decision will result in one or more of these conditions being true as to Familiar as
a result of the decision. (Regulation 18706.) Ms. Hodges' employer's only apparent connection to
the decision is the involvement of one of its customers, Granite. Your facts also indicate no
plumbing supplies are utilized in the project. One could argue that Ms. Hodges' vote on the
matter could either please or displease Granite, consequently making it more or less likely to
continue patronizing Ms. Hodges' employer. This, in turn, could cause Ms. Hodges' employer to
perceive her unfavorably if she votes the "wrong" way.

In the Stepanicich Advice Letter, No. A-96-217 we advised, "a mere perception is not
enough for the effect of a decision to be considered reasonably foreseeable." We went on to
warn, however, that if facts come to light that indicate the employer actually intends to alter the
employee-public official's employment status on the basis of a decision he or she makes, then
there would be a reasonably foreseeable effect on the employee-public official's interests. (Ibid.)

Absent facts which indicate that Familiar intends to take action about Ms. Hodges'
employment as a result of her vote on the Mid-Valley Parkway Project, the decision will not

5 Regulation 18704.1(a) states the conditions in which in a business entity, such as Familiar, is considered
to be directly involved in a governmental decision. None of these conditions are true as to Familiar in connection
with the Mid-Valley Parkway Project decisions. Therefore, Familiar is indirectly involved in the decision.
(Regulation 18704.1(b).)

File No. A-98-228
Page 6

have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Ms. Hodges' economic interests. Therefore,
she does not, at this time, have a conflict of interest arising from her employer in the Mid-Valley
Parkway Project vote.

Ms. Hodges must also consider whether the decision will have a personal financial effect
on her or on her immediate family.6 (Regulation 18703.5.) She may have a financial interest in
the decision if the reasonably foreseeable impact of the decision is that her personal expenses,
income, assets, liabilities, or those of her immediate family, will increase or decrease by at
least $250 in any 12-month period. (Regulation 18705.5.)

The above analysis focuses on the possibility of a financial effect on Familian, and
derivatively on Ms. Hodges' status as an employee. As to a personal financial effect, we have
been presented with no facts suggesting that such a personal financial effect on Ms. Hodges or
her immediate family could result other than through her employment. Therefore, the conclusion
that the vote on the Mid-Valley Parkway Project will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on her economic interest in her employer also holds true for the possibility of a personal
financial effect.

Finally, we note that although Granite is a substantial customer of her employer,
Ms. Hodges has received no commission income which is attributable to Granite. You do not
present facts indicating there is "promised" income from Granite to Ms. Hodges. Thus, Granite
is not a source of income to Ms. Hodges, nor otherwise her economic interest under the Act.7

C. Question (B): The subdivision map decision.

As explained above, Ms. Hodges has an economic interest in her employer, Familian.
Familian is indirectly involved in this decision, too; therefore, Regulation 18705.1(b)(7) again
provides the relevant materiality standard. The critical question is whether it is substantially
likely that the subdivision map decision will result in one of the conditions stated in subsection
(b)(7) being true as to Familian. (Regulation 18706.)

You have indicated that the developer will need to purchase plumbing supplies if the
subdivision map is approved and the project goes forward. You also tell us it is unknown where
the developer will purchase the supplies. From these facts, one could argue that the subdivision

_____________________
6 For purposes of the Act, "immediate family" means the spouse and dependent children. (Section
82029.)

7 We note that Ms. Hodges does not have an ownership interest in Familian Tile. Therefore, there is no
issue of income "passing through" the business entity to her. (See Section 82030, which prescribes that an
individual's income includes his/her pro rata share of the income of any business entity in which he/she owns 10
percent (or more).

File No. A-98-228
Page 7

map decision will have at least some reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Familian because
the project going forward would increase the local demand for plumbing supplies.

However, we have previously advised that a public official whose economic interests
have no previous connection to a project may participate in decisions about the project even if
he or she may later supply building materials to the project. (Guinan Advice Letter, No. A-94-
047.) This is especially true if the public official's business has relatively more, rather than less,
competition. (Strauss Advice Letter, No. A-96-034.)

Here, you have presented no facts indicating that Familian has a previous connection with
the project. If this is the case, although Familian may (or may not) supply the project's
contractors in the future, this possibility does not make it reasonably foreseeable at this point that
one of the materiality standards in Regulation 18705.1(b)(7) will be satisfied as a result of the
present decision. (Guinan, supra; Strauss, supra.) Thus, we advise that Ms. Hodges does not
presently have a disqualifying conflict of interest arising from her employer with regard to this
decision.

Ms. Hodges must consider whether the Council's decisions will have a "personal
financial effect" on her. (Regulations 18703.5, 18705.5.) As with the Mid-Valley Parkway
Project, we have been presented with no facts suggesting that such a personal financial effect on
Ms. Hodges could result other than through her employment. Therefore, the conclusion that the
vote on the subdivision map decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
her economic interest in her employer also holds true for the possibility of a personal financial
effect.

As explained above, Granite is not an economic interest of Ms. Hodges. Also, you have
presented no facts indicating that Ms. Hodges has any financial connection of the developer who
is applying for the subdivision map approval, either directly or through Familian. This being so,
Ms. Hodges does not have an economic interest in the developer.

D. Question (C): The Wessman DDA decision.

As explained above, Ms. Hodges has an economic interest in her employer, Familian.
Familian is indirectly involved in the Wessman DDA decision; therefore, Regulation
18705.1(b)(7) again provides the relevant materiality standard. The critical question is whether it
is substantially likely that the decision will result in one of the conditions stated in subsection
(b)(7) being true as to Familian. (Regulation 18706.)

Approving the DDA will, of course, facilitate the project moving forward, and if the
project moves forward, it will need plumbing supplies. Since Familian sells plumbing supplies,
it seems that there will be at least some financial impact on Familian. You have provided us with
two important facts on this issue. First, you tell us that Wessman has made no commitments to

File No. A-98-228
Page 8

purchase the necessary plumbing supplies from any particular vendor. Second, you tell us that
Wessman has been only an occasional customer of Familiar, and, indeed, Wessman typically
patronizes other vendors for plumbing supplies.

The Commission's interpretations and the Staff's advice on conflict of interest matters
have historically balanced a need for tough enforcement of the Act's conflict-of-interest
provisions with a realization that public officials operate in the real world where some business
interrelationships are inevitable. The key is identifying the interrelationships which present an
unacceptable risk of conflict of interest (i.e., those where the decision-in-question will have
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on a public official's economic interests) from
those which are incidental.

In the seminal Thorner Opinion (In re Thorner (1975) 1 F.P.P.C. Ops. 198), the
Commission considered a water district board's decisions to lift a moratorium on new water
connections; each decision would allow the construction of a particular project. One of the water
district board members had an economic interest in a business which sold to contractors who
were likely bidders on the projects that would go forward if the moratorium were lifted.

In Thorner, the Commission distinguished between bidders who were "regular
customer[s]... who normally buy[] principally or only from" the board member's business
and bidders who also make significant purchases from other vendors. The Commission
concluded that a decision to award a contract to a bidder who did not buy principally or only
from the board member's business was not substantially likely to have a material financial effect
on the board member's business.

Applying this law to your facts, you tell us that Wessman is only an occasional customer
of Familiar, and, indeed, typically buys plumbing supplies from other vendors. Based upon
these facts provided by you, we conclude that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the Wessman
DDA decision will result in a material financial effect on Familiar (i.e., it is not substantially
likely that one of the materiality standards in Regulation 18705.1(b)(7) will be true as a result of the decision). (Thorner, supra.) Therefore, Ms. Hodges does not, at this time, have a
conflict of interest arising from her employer in the Wessman DDA decision.

The next issue is whether Ms. Hodges has an economic interest in Wessman itself. Ms.
Hodges received commission income which is attributable to Wessman.8 However, the
amount of that income ($5.81 within the past twelve months) falls below the statutory threshold
($250 within twelve months) for establishing Wessman as an economic interest (i.e., source of

8 Regulation 18704.3 sets out rules for determining the source(s) of commission income. Pursuant to
subsections (c)(6)(B) and (d), the entire amount of Ms. Hodges' commission income from Wessman's account with
Familiar Title is attributable to him as its source, within the meaning of the Act.

File No. A-98-228
Page 9

income) of Ms. Hodges under the Act. (Regulation 18703.3(a).) Nor do you present facts
indicating there is "promised" income of $250. Thus, Wessman is not a source of income to
Ms. Hodges, nor otherwise her economic interest under the Act.⁹

Ms. Hodges must consider whether the Wessman DDA decision will have a "personal
financial effect" on her or her immediate family. As with the Mid-Valley Parkway Project and
the CUP decisions, we have been presented with no facts suggesting that such a personal
financial effect on Ms. Hodges could result other than through her employment. Therefore, the
conclusion that the vote on the Wessman DDA decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on her economic interest in her employer also holds true for the possibility of a
personal financial effect.

E. Question (D): the shopping center CUP decision.

As explained above, Ms. Hodges has an economic interest in her employer, Familian.
Familian is indirectly involved in the shopping center CUP decision; therefore, Regulation
18705.1(b)(7) again provides the relevant materiality standard. The critical question is whether it
is substantially likely that the decision will result in one of the conditions stated in subsection
(b)(7) being true as to Familian. (Regulation 18706.)

Our reasoning and conclusions with regard to this decision follow the reasoning and
conclusions explained above in the context of the Wessman DDA decision. As already
explained, Ms. Hodges does not have an economic interest in Wessman; therefore, no conflict of
interest arises for her a result of the decision's impact on Wessman. She has an economic
interest in her employer, but the employer's only connection to the shopping center project is that
Wessman is an occasional customer. Under Thorner, supra, the fact that the applicant is an
occasional or incidental customer of Familian is insufficient grounds to conclude that a material
financial effect on Familian is reasonably foreseeable.

_____________________________

⁹ We note that Ms. Hodges does not have an ownership interest in Familian Title. Therefore, there is no
issue of income "passing through" the business entity to her. (See Section 82030, which prescribes that an
individual's income includes his/her pro rata share of the income of any business entity in which he/she owns 10
percent (or more).

File No. A-98-228
Page 10

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By John Vergelli
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

inclusive:
“(A) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the gross
revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or
“(B) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year
in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
“(C) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of
assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more.”
The critical question is whether it is substantially likely that the Mid-Valley Parkway
Project decision will result in one or more of these conditions being true as to Farnilian as a
result of the decision. (Regulation 18706.) Ms. Hodges’ employer’s only apparent connection to
the decision is the involvement of one of its customers, Granite. Your facts also indicate no
plumbing supplies will be utilized in the project. One could argue that Ms. Hodges’ vote on the
matter could either please or displease Granite, consequently making it more or less likely to
continue patronizing Ms. Hodges’ employer. This, in turn, could cause Ms. Hodges’ employer to
perceive her unfavorably if she votes the “wrong” way.
In the Stepanicich Advice Letter, No. A-96-217, we advised, “a mere perception is not
enough for the effect of a decision to be considered reasonably foreseeable.” We went on to
warn, however, that if facts come to light that indicate the employer actually intends to alter the
employee-public official’s employment status on the basis of a decision he or she makes, then
there would be a reasonably foreseeable effect on the employee-public official’s interests. (Thid.)
Absent facts which indicate that Familian intends to take action about Ms. Hodges’
employment as a result of her vote on the Mid-Valley Parkway Project, the decision will not
Regulation 18704.1(a) states the conditions in which in a business entity, such as Familian, is considered
to be directly involved in a governmental decision. None of these conditions are true as to Familian in connection
with the Mid-Valley Parkway Project decisions. Therefore, Familian is indirectly involved in the decision.
(Regulation 18704.1(b).)
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Ms. Hodges’ economic interests. Therefore,
she does not, at this time, have a conflict of interest arising from her employer in the Mid-Valley
Parkway Project vote.
Ms. Hodges must also consider whether the decision will have a personal financial effect
on her or on her immediate family.
6
(Regulation 18703.5.) She may have a financial interest in
the decision if the reasonably foreseeable impact of the decision is that her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, or those ofher immediate family, will increase or decrease by at
least $250 in any 12-month period. (Regulation 18705.5.)
The above analysis focuses on the possibility of a financial effect on Familian, and
derivatively on Ms. Hodges’ status as an employee. As to a personal financial effect, we have
been presented with no facts suggesting that such a personal financial effect on Ms. Hodges or
her immediate family could result other than through her employment. Therefore, the conclusion
that the vote on the Mid-Valley Parkway Project will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on her economic interest in her employer also holds true for the possibility of a personal
financial effect.
Finally, we note that although Granite is a substantial customer of her employer,
Ms. Hodges has received no commission income which is attributable to Granite. You do not
present facts indicating there is “promised” income from Granite to Ms. Hodges. Thus, Granite
is not.a source of income to Ms. Hodges, nor otherwise her economic interest under the Act.
7
C. Question (B): The subdivision map decision.
As explained above, Ms. Hodges has an economic interest in her employer, Familian.
Familian is indirectly involved in this decision, too; therefore, Regulation 18705.1 (b)(7) again
provides the relevant materiality standard. The critical question is whether it is substantially
likely that the subdivision map decision will result in one of the conditions stated in subsection
(b)(7) being true as to Familian. (Regulation 18706.)
You have indicated that the developer will need to purchase plumbing supplies if the
subdivision map is approved and the project goes forward. You also tell us it is unknown where
the developer will purchase the supplies. From these facts, one could argue that the subdivision
6 For purposes of the Act, “immediate family” means the spouse and dependent children. (Section
82029.)
We note that Ms. Hodges does not have an ownership interest in Familian Tile. Therefore, there is no
issue of income “passing through” the business entity to her. (See Section 82030, which prescribes that an
individual’s income includes his/her pro rata share of the income of any business entity in which he/she owns 10
percent or more).
map decision will have at least some reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Familian because
the project going forward would increase the local demand for plumbing supplies.
However, we have previously advised that a public official whose economic interests
have no previous connection to a project may participate in decisions about the project even if
he or she may later supply building materials to the project. (Guinan Advice Letter, No. A-94-
047.) This is especially true if the public official’s business has relatively more, rather than less,
competition. (Strauss Advice Letter, No. A-96-034.)
Here, you have presented no facts indicating that Familian has a previous connection with
the project. If this is the case, although Familian may (or may not) supply the project’s
contractors in the future, this possibility does not make it reasonably foreseeable at this point that
one of the materiality standards in Regulation 18705.l(b)(7) will be satisfied as a result of the
present decision. (Guinan, supra; Strauss, supra.) Thus, we advise that Ms. Hodges does not
presently have a disqualifying conffict of interest arising from her employer with regard to this
decision.
Ms. Hodges must consider whether the Council’s decisions will have a “personal
financial effect” on her. (Regulations 18703.5, 18705.5.)
As with the Mid-Valley Parkway
Project, we have been presented with no facts suggesting that such a personal financial effect on
Ms. Hodges could result other than through her employment. Therefore, the conclusion that the
vote on the subdivision map decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
her economic interest in her employer also holds true for the possibility of a personal financial
effect.
As explained above, Granite is not an economic interest of Ms. Hodges. Also, you have
presented no facts indicating that Ms. Hodges has any financial connection ofthe developer who
is applying for the subdivision map approval, either directly or through Familian. This being so,
Ms. Hodges does not have an economic interest in the developer.
D. Question (C): The Wessman DDA decision.
As explained above, Ms. Hodges has an economic interest in her employer, Familian.
Familian is indirectly involved in the Wessman DDA decision; therefore, Regulation
18705.1 (b)(7) again provides the relevant materiality standard. The critical question is whether it
is substantially likely that the decision will result in one of the conditions stated in subsection
(b)(7) being true as to Familian. (Regulation 18706.)
Approving the DDA will, of course, facilitate the project moving forward, and if the
project moves forward, it will need plumbing supplies. Since Familian sells plumbing supplies,
it seems that there will be at least some financial impact on Familian. You have provided us with
two important facts on this issue. First, you tell us that Wessman has made no commitments to
purchase the necessary plumbing supplies from any particular vendor. Second, you tell us that
Wessman has been only an occasional customer of Familian, and, indeed, Wessman typically
patronizes other vendors for plumbing supplies.
The Commission’s interpretations and the Staffs advice on conflict of interest matters
have historically balanced a need for tough enforcement of the Act’s conflict-of-interest
provisions with a realization that public officials operate in the real world where some business
interrelationships are inevitable. The key is identifying the interrelationships which present an
unacceptable risk of conflict of interest (i.e., those where the decision-in-question will have
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on a public official’s economic interests) from
those which are incidental.
In the seminal Thorner Opinion (In re Thorner (1975) 1 F.P.P.C. Ops. 198), the
Commission considered a water district board’s decisions to lift a moratorium on new water
connections; each decision would allow the construction of a particular project. One ofthe water
district board members had an economic interest in a business which sold to contractors who
were likely bidders on the projects that would go forward if the moratorium were lifted.
In Thorner, the Commission distinguished between bidders who were “regular
customer[s]... who normally buy[j principally or only from” from the board member’s business
and bidders who also make significant purchases from other vendors.
The Commission
concluded that a decision to award a contract to a bidder who did not buy principally or only
from the board member’s business was not substantially likely to have a material financial effect
on the board member’s business.
Applying this law to your facts, you tell us that Wessman is only an occasional customer
ofFamilian, and, indeed, typically buys plumbing supplies from other vendors. Based upon
these facts provided by you, we conclude that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the Wessman
DDA decision will result in a material financial effect on Familian (i.e., it is not substantially
likely that one of the relevant materiality standards in Regulation 18705.1 (b)(7) will be true as a
result of the decision). (Thorner, supra.) Therefore, Ms. Hodges does not, at this time, have a
conffict of interest arising from her employer in the Wessman DDA decision.
The next issue is whether Ms. Hodges has an economic interest in Wessman itself. Ms.
Hodges has received commission income which is attributable to Wessman.
8 However, the
amount of that income ($5.81 within the past twelve months) falls below the statutory threshold
($250 within twelve months) for establishing Wessman as an economic interest (i.e., source of
Regulation 18704.3 sets out rules for determining the source(s) of commission income. Pursuant to
subsections (c)(6)(B) and (a), the entire amount ofMs. Hodges’ commission income from Wessman’s account with
Familian Tile is attributable to him as its source, within the meaning of the Act.
income) of Ms. Hodges under the Act. (Regulation 18703.3(a).) Nor do you present facts
indicating there is “promised” income of $250. Thus, Wessman is not a source of income to
Ms. Hodges, nor otherwise her economic interest under the Act.
9
Ms. Hodges must consider whether the Wessman DDA decision will have a “personal
financial effect” on her or her immediate family. As with the Mid-Valley Parkway Project and
the CUP decisions, we have been presented with no facts suggesting that such a personal
financial effect on Ms. Hodges could result other than through her employment. Therefore, the

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: (A) May Ms. Hodges take part in governmental decisions about selecting the general
contractor for the Mid-Valley Parkway Project, where Granite Construction, a significant
customer of Ms. Hodges’ employer, is bidding for the contract?
(B) Where Granite Construction is a contractor of a developer of a private shopping
center project, may Ms. Hodges take part in governmental decisions about the developer’s
application for a subdivision map and conditional use permit for the project (the “subdivision
map decision”)?
(C) Given that Wessman Development is an occasional customer of Ms. Hodges’
employer, may she take part in governmental decisions about a proposed disposition and
development agreement between Wessman Development and the City ofPalm Springs
Redevelopment Agency (the “Wessman DDA decision”)?Page 2
(0) Where Wessman Development owns land which it wishes to develop into a shopping
center, and has applied for a conditional use permit which would allow the development, may
Ms. Hodges take part in governmental decisions about the conditional use permit (the “shopping
center CUP decision”)?
II. CONCLUSIONS
Based upon the facts you have presented, Ms. Hodges does not at this time have a conflict
of interest with regard to any of these decisions about which you inquire.
HI. FACTS
2
Your office represents the City of Palm Springs (“City”) as its City Attorney, and are
authorized by Ms. Hodges to seek advice on her behalf..
Ms. Hodges is an employee of Faniilian Tile (“Familian”), a plumbing supply business.
She receives a set salary and commission income.
Granite Construction Company (“Granite”) is the largest contractor in the Coachella
Valley. Granite annually buys a significant amount ofplumbing products from Familian (in the
range of $4,000-$lOO,000 annually). Granite is not Ms. Hodges’ client and Ms. Hodges receives
no commission income from Granite.
Wessman Development (“Wessman”), a Palm Springs developer, has purchased $374
worth ofplumbing supplies from Familian in the last 12 months. Ms. Hodges has received a
nominal commission ($5.81) from Wessman for these sales. Although Wessman is an occasional
customer ofFamilian, you tell us that Wessman tends to use other vendors of plumbing supplies.
A. Facts specific to question (A).
Granite is bidding to be the general contractor on the Mid-Valley Parkway Project, which
is an approximately $15 million dollar regional street project administered by the City as lead
agency. The City must decide whether to award a bid to Granite as the general contractor for the
City. No plumbing supplies will be utilized in the project. The project will be competifively bid,
pursuant to state law.
2 The Commission does not act as a fmder of fact when it renders advice. This advice is applicable and
confers immunity (see Section 83114) only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct and that all of the
material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, 77.)
B. Facts specific to question (B).
Granite is the contractor building parking lot improvements on a private shopping center
project in the City. The developer who has hired Granite is seeking approval from the City ofthe
subdivision map for the shopping center project, and a conditional use permit (“CUP”) for the
development of the shopping center. The developer will purchase plumbing supplies for the
project; it is unknown where the developer will purchase the supplies. Wherever the plumbing
supplies are bought, they will not be bought or utilized by Granite.
C. Facts specific to question (C).
Wessman wishes to enter a disposition and development agreement (“DDA”) with the
Palm Springs Redevelopment Agency (“Agency”) for development of a shopping center, Plaza
Mercado, and a public parking lot thereon. The Agency plans to provide a subsidy to Wessman
in an amount of $500,000 toward the construction ofPlaza Mercado. The City Council will
consider the approval of the DDA between the Agency and Wessman for the project. Plumbing
supplies will be needed for the project, but it is unknown from whom Wessman will purchase the
supplies.
ft Facts specific to question (D).
Wessman seeks to develop a second shopping center on property it owns. The property is
entirely privately owned, and the City will not provide any assistance for the construction of the
shopping center. The City Council must decide whether to issue the CUP necessary for
development of the shopping center. Again, it is unknown where Wessman will purchase the
plumbing supplies for the project.

CONCLUSION: that the vote on the Wessman DDA decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on her economic interest in her employer also holds true for the possibility of a
personal financial effect.
E. Question (B): the shopping center CUP decision.
As explained above, Ms. Hodges has an economic interest in her employer, Familian.
Familian is indirectly involved in the shopping center CUP decision; therefore, Regulation
18705.1(b)(7) again provides the relevant materiality standard. The critical question is whether it
is substantially likely that the decision will result in one of the conditions stated in subsection
(b)(7) being true as to Familian. (Regulation 18706.)
Our reasoning and conclusions with regard to this decision follow the reasoning and
conclusions explained above in the context ofthe Wessman DDA decision. As already
explained, Ms. Hodges does not have an economic interest in Wessman; therefore, no conflict of
interest arises for her a result of the decision’s impact on Wessman. She has an economic
interest in her employer, but the employer’s only connection to the shopping center project is that
Wessman is an occasional customer. Under Thorner, supra, the fact that the applicant is an
occasional or incidental customer of Familian is insufficient grounds to conclude that a material
financial effect on Faniilian is reasonably foreseeable.
We note that Ms. Hodges does not have an ownership interest in Familian Tile. Therefore, there is no
issue of income “passing through” the business entity to her. (See Section 82030, which prescribes that an
individual’s income includes his/her pro rata share of the income of any business entity in which he/she owns 10
percent or more).

QUESTIONS (A) May Ms. Hodges take part in governmental decisions about selecting the general contractor for the Mid-Valley Parkway Project, where Granite Construction, a significant customer of Ms. Hodges' employer, is bidding for the contract? (B) Where Granite Construction is a contractor of a developer of a private shopping center project, may Ms. Hodges take part in governmental decisions about the developer's application for a subdivision map and conditional use permit for the project (the "subdivision map decision")? Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations. II. CONCLUSIONS Based upon the facts you have presented, Ms. Hodges does not at this time have a conflict of interest with regard to any of these decisions about which you inquire. III.
